title: ICSE Class X Physics Practice Paper 1
subtitle: Mechanics (Moments/Work), Electricity (Circuits/Safety), Sound (Echo/Pitch), Light (Refraction/TIR)
generalInstructions: Answers to this Paper must be written on the paper provided separately.
You will not be allowed to write during the first 15 minutes.
The time given at the head of this Paper is the time allowed for writing the answers.
Section A is compulsory. Attempt any four questions from Section B.
The intended marks for questions or parts of questions are given in brackets [ ].

questions.instruction: Choose the correct answers to the questions from the given options.

items.q: The energy conversion that occurs when a DC motor is switched ON is:
items.why_important: Tests the fundamental principle of the DC motor (Magnetism).
items.frequency: High (Core concept question, appears in theory sections every year: 2025 Specimen, School Prelims)

items.q: The refractive index of water with respect to air is $4/3$. The critical angle for the water-air interface is closest to:
items.why_important: Standard numerical on the relationship between $\mu$ and $C$ (Light/TIR).

items.q: The specific heat capacity of water is $4200 \text{ J/kg}^{\circ}\text{C}$. This means $4200 \text{ J}$ of heat is required to change the temperature of:
items.why_important: Tests the precise definition and units of Specific Heat Capacity.

items.q: Which radiation is uncharged and highly penetrating?
items.why_important: Tests the comparison of radioactive emissions (Modern Physics).
items.frequency: Frequent question on properties comparison (2025 Specimen, School Prelims)

items.q: If the amplitude of a sound wave is doubled, its loudness increases by a factor of:
items.why_important: Tests the relationship between Loudness and Amplitude (Loudness $\propto \text{Amplitude}^2$).
items.frequency: Standard concept in Sound (2018, School Prelims)

items.q: A $100 \text{ W}, 220 \text{ V}$ bulb is connected to a $220 \text{ V}$ supply. The resistance of the bulb is:
items.why_important: Standard numerical on calculating resistance from power rating ($R = V^2/P$).
items.frequency: Compulsory numerical in Electricity (2017, School Prelims)

items.q: A nut is opened using a wrench. This is an example of the moment of force acting as a:
items.why_important: Tests the fundamental definition and application of Moment of Force.

items.q: The phenomenon of light responsible for the apparent bending of a pencil partially immersed in water is:
items.why_important: Tests the observation and principle of refraction (Light).
items.frequency: Regularly appears as a concept question (School Prelims)

items.q: If the net force acting on a body is zero, the body is in:
items.why_important: Tests the precise definition of translational equilibrium (Mechanics/Moments).
items.frequency: Frequent concept question (2025 Specimen, School Prelims)

items.q: The S.I. unit of specific latent heat is:
items.why_important: Tests the correct S.I. unit for a key Heat quantity.

items.q: A pair of sugar tongs is an example of which class of lever?
items.why_important: Tests identification of lever class in real-life examples.
items.frequency: Common question on real-life levers (2017, School Prelims)

items.q: The colour of light that deviates the least when passing through a prism is:
If a resistor $R$ is connected across a battery of voltage $V$, the heat produced in time $t$ is given by:
items.why_important: Tests the mathematical expression for Joule's law (Electricity).

items.q: The principle of an AC Generator is:
items.why_important: Tests the core principle of the AC Dynamo.
items.frequency: Consistent core theory (2025 Specimen, School Prelims)

items.q: If the distance between a vibrating body and a reflector is $34 \text{ m}$, the time interval between the production and reception of the echo is:
items.why_important: Standard numerical on Echo (Speed of Sound $340 \text{ m/s}$).
items.frequency: Highly frequent numerical on Echo/Reverberation (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: A uniform metre rule is pivoted at the $0 \text{ cm}$ mark. It balances when a load of $50 \text{ gf}$ is suspended from the $70 \text{ cm}$ mark. Calculate the weight of the metre rule. [3]
parts.ans: $70 \text{ gf}$
parts.details:
Weight of rule $W$ acts at $50 	ext{ cm}$. Pivot at $0 	ext{ cm}$.
$	ext{Clockwise Moment} = 50 	ext{ gf} 	imes (70 - 0) = 3500 	ext{ gf} \cdot 	ext{cm}$.
$	ext{Anti-Clockwise Moment} = W 	imes (50 - 0) = 50W$. 
$50W = 3500 \implies W = 70 	ext{ gf}$.
parts.why_important: Core numerical on the Principle of Moments.
parts.frequency: Compulsory numerical in Section A (2017, School Prelims)

parts.text: The power of a lens is $-5 \text{ D}$. State the nature of the lens and the type of image (real/virtual) it forms for all positions of the object. [3]
parts.ans: Nature: Concave (Diverging) Lens. Image Type: Virtual Image for all positions of the object.
parts.details: Negative power implies a concave lens. Concave lenses always form virtual, erect, and diminished images.
parts.why_important: Tests the properties and sign convention of a concave lens.
parts.frequency: Standard short question on lenses (2025 Specimen, School Prelims)

parts.text: State two ways in which the frequency of a sound wave produced by a vibrating stretched string can be increased. [2]
parts.ans: 1. Increase the Tension ($T$) in the string ($f \propto \sqrt{T}$). 2. Decrease the Length ($L$) of the vibrating segment ($f \propto 1/L$).
parts.details: Checks the factors affecting frequency (Sonometer Laws).
parts.why_important: Core theory from the Sound chapter.
parts.frequency: Very frequent factors question (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: A block and tackle system with a Velocity Ratio of 5 is $80\text{\%}$ efficient. Calculate its Mechanical Advantage (MA). [3]
parts.details: Efficiency $\eta = \frac{\text{MA}}{\text{VR}}$. $\text{MA} = \eta \times \text{VR} = 0.80 \times 5 = 4$.
parts.why_important: Numerical application of the MA-VR-Efficiency relationship.
parts.frequency: Compulsory numerical in Mechanics (2017, School Prelims)

parts.text: What is the function of the fuse in a household electrical circuit? Why is it connected in the Live wire? [3]
parts.ans: Function: The fuse is a safety device that melts and breaks the circuit when an excessively high current flows (due to short circuit or overload). Live wire connection: It must be connected in the Live wire to ensure that the entire appliance is completely isolated from the high-potential supply when the fuse blows, preventing shock.
parts.details: Checks safety knowledge and the reason for the fuse's placement.
parts.why_important: Compulsory safety reasoning question (Electricity/House Wiring).

parts.text: Define the S.I. unit of energy in terms of the S.I. unit of force and distance. [2]
parts.ans: The S.I. unit of energy is the **Joule (J)**. One Joule is the energy expended (or work done) when a force of **one Newton (N)** displaces a body through **one metre (m)** in the direction of the force. $\mathbf{1 \text{ J} = 1 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m}}$.
parts.details: Checks the definition of Joule and its relation to work ($W=F \cdot d$).
parts.why_important: Fundamental definition of a key concept (W.E.P.).

parts.text: An object is placed at $2F_1$ of a convex lens. Draw a neat, labelled ray diagram to show the formation of the image. State two characteristics of the image. [4]
parts.ans: Diagram shows: Object at $2F_1$, rays passing through the lens, image formed at $2F_2$. Characteristics: Real, Inverted, Same size as the object.
parts.details: Tests the ability to draw a fundamental ray diagram for a convex lens and state its properties.
parts.why_important: Compulsory Ray Diagram question (Light).
parts.frequency: Highly frequent diagram question (2024 School Prelims, 2025 Specimen Pattern)

parts.text: The velocity of a car changes from $10 \text{ m/s}$ to $20 \text{ m/s}$. How does the kinetic energy of the car change (i.e., by what factor)? [2]
parts.ans: The Kinetic Energy increases by a factor of $\mathbf{4}$ (or becomes 4 times the original KE).
parts.details: Kinetic Energy $\propto \text{velocity}^2$. $\text{New KE} \propto (20)^2 = 400$. $\text{Old KE} \propto (10)^2 = 100$. $\text{Factor} = 400/100 = 4$.
parts.why_important: Core concept of W.E.P. (KE $\propto v^2$).
parts.frequency: Regularly appears as a concept question (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: State the difference between an **echo** and a **reverberation**. Why are the walls of concert halls covered with thick curtains? [3]
parts.ans: Difference: Echo is a distinct, separate repetition of sound (from a distant reflector). Reverberation is the prolonged persistence of sound due to multiple, rapid, overlapping reflections (from nearby surfaces). Curtains: To **reduce reverberation**. Thick curtains are good sound absorbers, preventing the multiple reflections that cause sound to persist and become unclear.
parts.details: Checks the difference between the two sound phenomena and a key application of acoustics.
parts.why_important: Standard theory/reasoning pair in the Sound chapter.
parts.frequency: Very frequent theory/reasoning question (2018, School Prelims)

sectionB.instruction: Attempt any four questions from this Section.

parts.text: A uniform plank of mass $40 \text{ kg}$ is $4 \text{ m}$ long. It is supported at two points $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$, which are at the two ends. A load of $60 \text{ kgf}$ is placed at $1 \text{ m}$ from end $\text{A}$.
parts.ans: 1. $35 \text{ kgf}$. 2. $65 \text{ kgf}$.
parts.details: Plank Weight $W_P = 40 \text{ kgf}$ acts at $2 \text{ m}$ from $\text{A}$. Load $L = 60 \text{ kgf}$ acts at $1 \text{ m}$ from $\text{A}$. \n1. Taking moments about $\text{A}$: $\text{Clockwise Moment} = W_P \times 2 + L \times 1 = 40 \times 2 + 60 \times 1 = 140 \text{ kgf} \cdot \text{m}$. $\text{Anti-Clockwise Moment} = R_B \times 4$. $4 R_B = 140 \implies R_B = 35 \text{ kgf}$.\n2. $\text{Total Downward Force} = 40 + 60 = 100 \text{ kgf}$. $\text{Total Upward Force} = R_A + R_B$. $R_A + 35 = 100 \implies R_A = 65 \text{ kgf}$.
parts.why_important: High-weightage application numerical on the Principle of Moments and non-uniform loading (Statics).
parts.frequency: Compulsory numerical in Section B (2017, School Prelims)

parts.text: A boy climbs a flight of stairs $10 \text{ m}$ high in $20 \text{ seconds}$. His power is $400 \text{ W}$. Calculate the mass of the boy. $(g = 10 \text{ N/kg})$ [4]
parts.ans: $80 \text{ kg}$
parts.details: Power $P = \frac{\text{Work}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{m g h}{t}$. $m = \frac{P \cdot t}{g \cdot h} = \frac{400 \text{ W} \times 20 \text{ s}}{10 \text{ N/kg} \times 10 \text{ m}} = \frac{8000}{100} = 80 \text{ kg}$.
parts.why_important: Application numerical on Power in W.E.P. (often Q1 but can be extended).

parts.text: Three resistors, $4 \text{ \Omega}$, $6 \text{ \Omega}$, and $12 \text{ \Omega}$, are connected in parallel to a $12 \text{ V}$ battery.
sub: Calculate the equivalent resistance of the circuit. [2]
Calculate the total current drawn from the battery. [2]
Calculate the current flowing through the $6 \text{ \Omega}$ resistor. [2]
parts.ans: 1. $2 \text{ \Omega}$. 2. $6 \text{ A}$. 3. $2 \text{ A}$.
parts.details: 1. $\frac{1}{R_p} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{12} = \frac{3+2+1}{12} = \frac{6}{12} = \frac{1}{2}$. $R_p = 2 \text{ \Omega}$.\n2. $I_{\text{total}} = V/R_p = 12 \text{ V} / 2 \text{ \Omega} = 6 \text{ A}$.\n3. $\text{Current in } 6 \text{ \Omega}$ resistor: $I_2 = V/R_2 = 12 \text{ V} / 6 \text{ \Omega} = 2 \text{ A}$.
parts.why_important: Compulsory complex numerical on Parallel/Series circuits (a high-weightage format).
parts.frequency: Highly frequent and high-weightage numerical (2018, 2025 Specimen)

parts.text: State the use of a two-way switch in house wiring. Give two advantages of the ring system of wiring. [4]
parts.ans: Use: To control a single electrical device (usually a light) from two different positions (e.g., in a staircase, commonly known as staircase wiring). Advantages: 1. Allows smaller diameter wires to be used (lower current in each wire). 2. Enables appliances to continue working from the other side of the ring if a fault occurs.
parts.details: Checks knowledge of practical wiring components and system advantages.
parts.why_important: Core theory on House Wiring and Safety.

parts.text: A person standing $49.5 \text{ m}$ away from a wall claps and hears the echo $0.3 \text{ s}$ later.
sub: Calculate the speed of sound in air. [2]
If the air temperature changes and the speed of sound increases to $340 \text{ m/s}$, calculate the new time interval for the echo. [2]
parts.details: 1. $\text{Speed } v = \frac{2d}{t} = \frac{2 \times 49.5 \text{ m}}{0.3 \text{ s}} = 330 \text{ m/s}$.\n2. $\text{New Time } t' = \frac{2d}{v'} = \frac{2 \times 49.5 \text{ m}}{340 \text{ m/s}} = \frac{99}{340} \approx 0.291 \text{ s}$.
parts.why_important: Numerical application of the Echo principle (Sound).
parts.frequency: Highly frequent numerical on Echo (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: Distinguish between **pitch** and **loudness** of sound based on the **objective** properties of the wave. [4]
parts.ans: Pitch: The subjective property that depends on the **frequency** (Objective) of the wave. Loudness: The subjective property that depends on the **amplitude** (Objective) of the wave.
parts.details: Checks the objective-subjective relationship of sound properties.
parts.why_important: Core theory on Sound properties (essential distinction).

parts.text: A glass tumbler suddenly cracks when a singer hits a high note. Name and define the phenomenon. [2]
parts.ans: Phenomenon: **Resonance**. Definition: The special case of forced vibration where the frequency of the applied external force (singer's note) is exactly equal to the natural frequency of the body (glass tumbler), leading to vibrations of maximum amplitude, causing the crack.
parts.details: Tests the application of Resonance in a real-life example.
parts.why_important: Application-based theory on Resonance.

parts.text: Define the **angle of deviation** for a prism. State two ways to reduce the angle of deviation produced by a prism. [3]
parts.ans: Deviation: The angle between the direction of the incident ray and the direction of the emergent ray (for a prism). Ways to Reduce: 1. Decrease the angle of the prism ($A$). 2. Choose a material with a lower refractive index ($\mu$). 3. Decrease the angle of incidence ($i$) towards the minimum deviation position.
parts.details: Checks definition and factors affecting deviation.
parts.why_important: Core theory on Prism (Light).

parts.text: The speed of light in a liquid is $2.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$. The speed of light in a vacuum is $3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate: 1. The refractive index of the liquid. [2] 2. The critical angle for the liquid-air boundary. [3]
parts.why_important: Numerical application of $\mu$ definition and TIR concept.

parts.text: Give one reason why a thin convex lens is preferred over a thick convex lens of the same focal length. [2]
parts.ans: A thin lens has less spherical and chromatic aberration (defects) compared to a thick lens, making the image sharper and clearer.
parts.details: Checks knowledge of lens imperfections (Light).
parts.why_important: Reasoning question on lens defects.

parts.text: $10 \text{ g}$ of steam at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ is passed into $100 \text{ g}$ of water at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$. Find the final temperature of the mixture.
sub: Calculate the maximum heat that can be released by the steam if it condenses and cools to the final temperature $T$. [4]
Calculate the final temperature of the mixture $T$. [3]
parts.ans: Final Temperature $\mathbf{100^{\circ}\text{C}}$ (with some steam remaining).
parts.details: 1. $\text{Heat Gained to reach } 100^{\circ}\text{C}$ by water: $Q_{\text{gain}} = m_w c_w \Delta T = 100 \times 4.2 \times (100 - 20) = 33600 \text{ J}$. $\text{Heat Lost by } 10 \text{ g}$ steam to condense: $Q_{\text{steam condenses}} = m_{\text{steam}} L_{\text{vap}} = 10 \times 2268 = 22680 \text{ J}$. Since $Q_{\text{gain}} (33600 \text{ J}) > Q_{\text{steam condenses}} (22680 \text{ J})$, the steam will condense completely, and the final temperature will be $\mathbf{100^{\circ}\text{C}}$ with some steam remaining to reach equilibrium.
parts.why_important: Challenging numerical on Steam Calorimetry, checking phase change calculations.
parts.frequency: Highly frequent and high-weightage numerical (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: Explain why food cooks faster in a pressure cooker. [3]
parts.ans: In a pressure cooker, the pressure above the water increases due to the sealed container. This increased pressure **raises the boiling point of water** above $100^{\circ}\text{C}$. The food is thus cooked at a higher temperature, which accelerates the cooking process.
parts.details: Checks the application of the effect of pressure on boiling point.
parts.why_important: Standard reasoning question in Calorimetry.
parts.frequency: Regularly appears as a 'Give Reason' question (School Prelims)

parts.text: An element ${}\*{Z}^{A}\text{X}$ undergoes a decay process to form an element ${}\*{82}^{206}\text{Pb}$ by the emission of two alpha particles and one beta particle. Determine the atomic number $(Z)$ and mass number $(A)$ of the element $\text{X}$. [4]
parts.details: Mass Number $A$: $A = 206 + (2 \times 4) + (1 \times 0) = 206 + 8 = 214$. Atomic Number $Z$: $Z = 82 + (2 \times 2) + (1 \times (-1)) = 82 + 4 - 1 = 85$. **Correction based on common error/simplified calculation:** $Z=82 + 4 - 1 = 85$. **Actual Answer $Z=85, A=214$**. (The previous prompt example had $83/214$. I will stick to the correct derivation: $Z=85, A=214$)
parts.why_important: Challenging numerical on balancing nuclear equations (Modern Physics).
parts.frequency: Highly frequent and high-weightage numerical (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: State three properties that are common to all electromagnetic waves. [3]
parts.ans: 1. All are transverse waves. 2. All travel at the speed of light ($3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$) in a vacuum. 3. All are uncharged and hence are not deflected by electric or magnetic fields. 4. All carry energy and momentum.
parts.details: Checks the fundamental properties of EM Waves.
parts.why_important: Core theory on EM Spectrum.

parts.text: Give one major difference between nuclear fission and nuclear fusion. [3]
parts.ans: Fission: A heavy nucleus (e.g., Uranium) splits into two smaller nuclei. Fusion: Two light nuclei (e.g., Hydrogen isotopes) combine to form a heavier nucleus.
parts.details: Checks the distinction between the two processes.
parts.why_important: Core distinction between the two nuclear processes.

parts.text: A block and tackle system has a Velocity Ratio of 5. If the load is $1000 \text{ N}$ and the effort applied is $250 \text{ N}$, is the machine a force multiplier? Is the machine a speed multiplier? Justify your answers. [4]
parts.ans: Force Multiplier: Yes. Justification: $\text{MA} = 1000/250 = 4$. Since $\text{MA} > 1$, it is a force multiplier. Speed Multiplier: No. Justification: Since $\text{MA} < \text{VR}$, it is not an ideal machine. For a force multiplier, the distance moved by the effort is always greater than the distance moved by the load, meaning $\text{VR} > 1$, so it is a **distance-decreasing** and thus **speed-decreasing** machine.
parts.details: Tests the properties of force/speed multiplication for a practical machine.
parts.why_important: Application of MA/VR principles (Moments/Pulleys).

parts.text: An object is placed $10 \text{ cm}$ from a convex lens of focal length $15 \text{ cm}$. Calculate the position and magnification of the image formed. [4]
parts.ans: Position: $-30 \text{ cm}$. Magnification: $+3$.
parts.why_important: Numerical on the convex lens forming a virtual image (Magnifying glass case).

parts.text: Distinguish between a real image and a virtual image (two points). [2]
parts.ans: Real Image: Can be obtained on a screen; rays actually meet. Virtual Image: Cannot be obtained on a screen; rays only appear to meet.
parts.details: Checks the fundamental distinction between the two image types (Light).
parts.why_important: Core theory in Light.
